spec.reasoning: I chose lexical renames and a distractor column. Column renames change surface anchors: 'Line' → 'Order Line', 'Order issued Date' → 'Issue Date', 'Request del date' → 'Req Delivery Date', 'Order Qty' → 'Ordered Qty', 'Expected outcome' → 'Allocation Result', 'Incoming Qty' → 'Available Stock Qty', and 'Item code' → 'Part No'. These are semantically equivalent at the same descriptiveness level. I did NOT rename 'Order No' or 'Item' because both appear as common English words/phrases heavily used in the instruction body (e.g., 'Order No. 1001-A', 'same item'). Sheet names are kept unchanged to avoid the multi_sheet_references risk flag — the instruction explicitly references 'Sheet1' and 'Sheet2', and renaming them would require updating the instruction text (which we avoid since instruction_paraphrase is false). No blank row insert is used because the previous attempt failed due to shifting Sheet2 data and breaking cross-sheet lookups. Instead, a distractor column 'Shipment Status' is added after the last column on Sheet1, which is plausibly related to an orders report but clearly distinct from any existing column.
